data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-06T01:21:27.775Z</current_datetime>

I have this data-driven test using object arrays. Is there a better way?

```csharp
[TestMethod]
[DynamicData(nameof(TestData))]
public void Validate_ReturnsExpected(string input, bool expected)
{
    var result = Validator.IsValid(input);
    Assert.AreEqual(expected, result);
}

public static IEnumerable<object[]> TestData =>
[
    ["valid@email.com", true],
    ["invalid", false],
    ["", false],
];
```

data.content:
Yes — for simple inline constants, use `[DataRow]` directly. It's more concise and gives you named test cases in the runner:

```csharp
[TestMethod]
[DataRow("valid@email.com", true, DisplayName = "Valid email")]
[DataRow("invalid", false, DisplayName = "Missing @")]
[DataRow("", false, DisplayName = "Empty string")]
public void Validate_ReturnsExpected(string input, bool expected)
{
    var result = Validator.IsValid(input);
    Assert.AreEqual(expected, result);
}
```

**When to use which:**

| Approach | Use when |
|----------|----------|
| `[DataRow]` | Compile-time constants, simple args |
| `[DynamicData]` | Runtime-generated data, complex objects, or shared across tests |

If you stay with `DynamicData` but want type safety, consider returning `IEnumerable<(string, bool)>` with MSTest 3.x+ which supports tuple unwrapping, or use a strongly-typed test helper:

```csharp
public static IEnumerable<object[]> TestData =>
    new (string Input, bool Expected)[]
    {
        ("valid@email.com", true),
        ("invalid", false),
        ("", false),
    }.Select(t => new object[] { t.Input, t.Expected });
```

For your case, `[DataRow]` is the cleanest fit since all values are constants.
data.reasoningText:
The user is asking about a better way to write data-driven tests in MSTest instead of using `object[]` arrays with `DynamicData`. The main downsides of `object[]` are lack of type safety and poor test case naming in output.

I can suggest using `[DataRow]` for simple inline cases, or for more complex scenarios, using strongly-typed tuples or a custom data source. Let me give a concise answer.
